The critical (sensitive) period hypothesis proposes that:

choices.A: language can be acquired equally easily at any age.
choices.B: there is a developmental window during which language is acquired most readily.

items.explanation: The hypothesis posits an optimal developmental window for ready language acquisition.

QUESTION:
The babbling stage is notable because it:

choices.B: precedes the production of meaningful words.
choices.D: is unique to children learning written language.

items.explanation: Babbling is pre-linguistic vocal play that comes before meaningful words.

items.question: Accumulated knowledge and verbal skills that tend to increase with age and experience reflect:
items.explanation: Crystallized intelligence is accumulated knowledge and verbal ability, which typically grows with experience; fluid intelligence is reasoning about novel problems.

items.question: A reinforcement schedule that delivers a reward after a fixed number of responses is a:
items.explanation: A fixed-ratio schedule reinforces after a set number of responses and produces high, steady response rates.

items.question: Cooley's concept of the "looking-glass self" proposes that we develop our self-concept based on:

choices.B: how we imagine others perceive and judge us.

items.explanation: The looking-glass self holds that self-concept forms through our perception of how others see and judge us.

items.question:
PASSAGE:
We have learned to speak of attention as a resource: something to be managed, captured, spent, and—by those who design the screens we look at—harvested and sold. The phrase "attention economy" captures something real. Attention is finite; every glance given to one thing is withheld from another, and there are now vast enterprises devoted to securing as much of it as they can. As far as it goes, the framing is sound. It does not go far enough.
For attention is not only a quantity to be allocated; it is also a way of valuing. To attend to something is already to treat it as worth attending to, and the manner of our attention—careless or careful, fleeting or sustained—partly determines what the thing becomes for us. A landscape glanced at is scenery; the same landscape studied becomes a place. A person half-heard is an obstacle or an audience; a person truly listened to is, for that span of time, more fully present in the world. What we attend to, and how, is therefore not a neutral matter of resource management. It is closer to a continual, half-conscious verdict on what matters.
This is why the deepest worry about the attention economy is not the obvious one. The obvious worry is distraction: that our focus is fractured and our minds scattered. The graver worry is the outsourcing of the verdict itself—that we increasingly let systems built to hold our gaze decide where it falls, and so let them decide, on our behalf and largely without our noticing, what we will treat as mattering. To reclaim attention, on this view, is not merely to concentrate harder. It is to take back the choice of what deserves it.

QUESTION:
The central claim of the passage is that attention is:

choices.A: merely a finite resource to be managed efficiently.
choices.B: not only a resource but a way of valuing, so that ceding control of it means ceding control over what we treat as mattering.
choices.C: unlimited and therefore not worth protecting.
choices.D: irrelevant to how we experience the world.

items.explanation: The thesis (paragraph 2) is that attention is "also a way of valuing," and paragraph 3 warns that ceding it means letting others "decide... what we will treat as mattering." (A) is the partial view the author extends; (C) and (D) contradict the text.

QUESTION:
The author's attitude toward the "attention economy" framing is that it is:

choices.A: entirely mistaken and should be rejected.
choices.B: sound as far as it goes, but incomplete.
choices.C: the deepest possible account of attention.

items.explanation: The author calls the framing "sound" but immediately adds "It does not go far enough"—useful but incomplete.

QUESTION:
The contrast between a landscape "glanced at" and one "studied" is used to illustrate that:

choices.A: landscapes are more valuable than people.
choices.B: the manner of our attention partly determines what a thing becomes for us.
choices.C: attention has no effect on perception.
choices.D: studying a landscape is a waste of a finite resource.

items.explanation: The glanced-at/studied contrast shows that "the manner of our attention... partly determines what the thing becomes for us." (A) misreads an illustration as a ranking.

QUESTION:
According to the passage, the "graver worry" about the attention economy is:

choices.A: that our focus is fractured and scattered.
choices.B: that we outsource to designed systems the choice of what we attend to, and thus what we treat as mattering.
choices.D: that screens are difficult to design.

items.explanation: The passage distinguishes the "obvious worry" (distraction) from the "graver worry": "the outsourcing of the verdict itself"—letting systems decide where our attention falls and thus what matters.

QUESTION:
Based on the final paragraph, "reclaiming attention" means primarily:

choices.A: learning to concentrate for longer periods.
choices.B: taking back the choice of what deserves one's attention.
choices.C: spending less time on all activities.
choices.D: allowing systems to allocate attention more efficiently.

items.explanation: The closing sentence defines reclaiming attention as taking "back the choice of what deserves it," explicitly "not merely to concentrate harder."

items.question:
PASSAGE:
The heart is a four-chambered pump that drives two circuits. In the pulmonary circuit, the right side of the heart pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs; oxygenated blood returns to the left atrium via the pulmonary veins. In the systemic circuit, the left side pumps oxygenated blood to the rest of the body.
The heartbeat is initiated by the sinoatrial (SA) node, a cluster of specialized cells that act as the heart's natural pacemaker, generating the electrical impulses that set the rhythm. The cardiac cycle alternates between contraction (systole) and relaxation (diastole). Valves between the atria and ventricles, and between the ventricles and the great arteries, ensure that blood flows in only one direction.

QUESTION:
The sinoatrial (SA) node functions as the heart's:
items.explanation: The SA node spontaneously depolarizes and sets the heart's rhythm—the natural pacemaker.

QUESTION:
Oxygenated blood returning from the lungs enters the heart at the:
items.explanation: Pulmonary veins deliver oxygenated blood to the left atrium, which passes it to the left ventricle for systemic distribution.

QUESTION:
The pulmonary circuit carries blood between the:
items.explanation: The pulmonary circuit runs between the heart and lungs; the systemic circuit serves the body's tissues.

QUESTION:
The heart valves function primarily to:

choices.B: prevent the backflow of blood, ensuring one-way flow.

items.explanation: Valves enforce one-way flow, preventing backflow between chambers and into the heart from the great arteries.

QUESTION:
During ventricular systole, the:

choices.A: ventricles relax and fill with blood.
choices.B: ventricles contract and eject blood while the atrioventricular valves close.
choices.D: atria contract while the ventricles remain empty.

items.explanation: In ventricular systole the ventricles contract to eject blood; the AV valves close (producing the first heart sound) to prevent backflow into the atria.

items.question: Mature mammalian red blood cells are specialized for oxygen transport and notably:

choices.C: are the largest cells in the body.

items.explanation: Mammalian RBCs extrude their nucleus (and most organelles), maximizing space for hemoglobin and giving their biconcave shape.

items.question: Bile, which emulsifies dietary fats to aid their digestion, is produced by the:
items.explanation: The liver produces bile (stored and concentrated in the gallbladder), which emulsifies fats.

items.question: A testcross—breeding an individual of unknown genotype with a homozygous recessive individual—is used to determine:

choices.A: the phenotype of the offspring only.
choices.B: whether an organism showing the dominant phenotype is homozygous or heterozygous.
choices.D: the mutation rate of a gene.

items.explanation: Crossing with a homozygous recessive reveals the unknown genotype: any recessive-phenotype offspring indicate the dominant parent was heterozygous.

items.question:
PASSAGE:
In mechanics, work is done when a force acts over a displacement: W = Fd cos θ, where θ is the angle between the force and the displacement. A force perpendicular to the motion does no work. The work–energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy. Power is the rate of doing work, P = W/t.
Energy can be stored as gravitational potential energy, PE = mgh, and converted between potential and kinetic forms; in the absence of friction, total mechanical energy is conserved.

QUESTION:
A force acts on an object at a right angle (90°) to its direction of motion. The work done by that force is:

choices.D: equal to the force times the distance.

items.explanation: W = Fd cos θ; at θ = 90°, cos 90° = 0, so the work is zero (e.g., the centripetal force in uniform circular motion does no work).

QUESTION:
According to the work–energy theorem, the net work done on an object equals its change in:
items.explanation: The work–energy theorem equates net work with the change in kinetic energy.

QUESTION:
Power is best defined as:

choices.B: the rate at which work is done.
choices.C: the force applied to an object.

items.explanation: Power is work per unit time (P = W/t), measured in watts.

QUESTION:
Lifting a box straight up to a higher shelf increases the box's:
items.explanation: Raising the box increases its height h, so its gravitational potential energy (mgh) rises.

QUESTION:
A constant 10 N force pushes a box 5 m in the same direction as the force. The work done is:
Two identical resistors are connected in parallel. The total (equivalent) resistance of the combination is:

choices.A: twice the value of one resistor.
choices.C: half the value of one resistor.

items.explanation: For two equal resistors in parallel, R_eq = R/2; parallel combinations always give less resistance than the smallest branch.

items.question: Which of the following is a strong base?
items.explanation: NaOH dissociates completely into Na⁺ and OH⁻, a strong base; ammonia and acetic acid are weak, and water is essentially neutral.

items.question: Two molecules that are non-superimposable mirror images of each other are called:
items.explanation: Non-superimposable mirror images are enantiomers, a type of stereoisomer.

items.question:
PASSAGE:
Sociologists study social movements—organized, collective efforts to promote or resist social change. Movements are often classified by the scope of change they seek: a reform movement seeks limited change within the existing social order, whereas a revolutionary movement seeks to overturn and replace that order.
Several theories address why movements arise and succeed. Relative deprivation theory holds that movements emerge when people perceive a gap between what they have and what they believe they deserve or what others have—deprivation judged relative to expectations rather than in absolute terms. Resource mobilization theory shifts the focus to the practical means of organizing: a movement's success depends heavily on its access to resources such as funding, leadership, and organizational structure.

QUESTION:
A social movement that seeks limited change within the existing social order is best classified as a:
items.explanation: A reform movement pursues limited change while leaving the broader social order intact.

items.question:
PASSAGE:

QUESTION:
Relative deprivation theory holds that movements arise when people perceive that:

choices.A: they are deprived relative to their expectations or to others.
choices.B: they have more than enough resources.

items.explanation: Relative deprivation is a perceived gap between one's situation and one's expectations or others' circumstances—deprivation judged relatively, not absolutely.

choices.B: access to resources such as funding, leadership, and organization.

items.explanation: Resource mobilization theory centers on practical resources—money, leadership, organization—as drivers of movement success.

QUESTION:
A movement aiming to overturn and replace the entire existing social or political order is a:
items.explanation: A revolutionary movement seeks to replace the existing order entirely.

Resource mobilization theory shifts the focus to the practical means of organizing: a movement's success depends heavily on its access to resources such as funding, leadership, and organizational structure.

QUESTION:
A finding that movements with stronger organization and funding are more likely to achieve their goals most directly supports:
items.explanation: Linking success to organizational resources is the core prediction of resource mobilization theory.

items.question: Drive-reduction theory explains motivation as the tendency to:

choices.A: seek out increasing levels of arousal.
choices.B: act to reduce internal tension and restore physiological balance.

items.explanation: Drive-reduction theory holds that an unmet need creates a drive (tension) that behavior acts to reduce, restoring homeostasis.

items.question: Selye's General Adaptation Syndrome describes the body's response to prolonged stress in the sequence:
items.explanation: The GAS sequence is alarm (initial reaction), resistance (sustained coping), and exhaustion (resources depleted).

items.question: Max Weber characterized the modern organizational form defined by hierarchy, formal rules, and impersonality as a:
items.explanation: Weber's ideal-type bureaucracy is marked by hierarchy, explicit rules, and impersonal, merit-based operation.

items.question:
PASSAGE:
We tend to think of ritual as the husk of belief. First, the story goes, people believe something—that the dead live on, that a marriage is sealed in heaven, that bread can become more than bread—and the ritual is merely the outward enactment of that inner conviction. It follows that once the belief is gone, the ritual is empty: a hollow performance that the honest person, having seen through it, ought to set aside. To keep the form without the faith looks like hypocrisy, or at best a sentimental clinging to what one no longer means.
But this gets the matter half backward. Ritual does work that belief, by itself, does not. It gives shape to feelings too large or too formless to be managed alone—the grief that has no words, the gratitude that would otherwise dissipate. It marks the thresholds of a life—birth, coming of age, marriage, death—that might otherwise slip by unacknowledged, as though nothing had changed. And it binds people together by the simple fact of their doing the same thing, at the same time, side by side. None of these functions requires that the participants assent to a particular doctrine; they are accomplished in the doing, not in the believing.
This is not to reduce ritual to a useful fiction, a machinery of social cohesion that the knowing may operate without sincerity. That account is as crude as the one it replaces, for it assumes that anything not literally believed must be either deception or mere technique. The truth lies outside that dichotomy. A ritual can mean something without asserting anything—much as a handshake means something, conveys and even creates a relation, without stating a proposition that could be true or false. To ask whether one "believes in" a handshake is to have misunderstood what kind of thing it is.

QUESTION:
The central claim of the passage is that:

choices.A: ritual is merely the outward husk of belief and should be discarded once belief fades.
choices.B: ritual does work independent of belief, and the "sincere belief vs. empty performance" dichotomy is too crude.
choices.C: ritual is a useful fiction that informed people may perform insincerely.
choices.D: all rituals assert propositions that are either true or false.

items.explanation: The passage argues ritual "does work that belief, by itself, does not" and that "the truth lies outside" the believe-or-pretend dichotomy. (A) and (C) are the two views the author rejects; (D) is contradicted by the handshake point.

The author presents the view that ritual is "the husk of belief" in order to:

choices.A: endorse it as the correct account.

The functions the author lists in the second paragraph (shaping feeling, marking thresholds, binding people) primarily serve to show that ritual:

Why does the author reject the view that ritual is "a useful fiction... operated without sincerity"?

choices.A: Because that view correctly captures all ritual.

items.question:
PASSAGE:
Gas exchange in the lungs occurs across the walls of the alveoli, tiny air sacs surrounded by capillaries. Exchange occurs by passive diffusion: each gas moves down its own partial-pressure gradient. Because the partial pressure of oxygen (PO₂) in freshly inhaled alveolar air is higher than in the deoxygenated blood arriving from the body, O₂ diffuses from the alveoli into the blood; CO₂, at higher partial pressure in the blood, diffuses the other way.
Two features make this exchange efficient: the alveoli collectively provide an enormous surface area, and the alveolar–capillary membrane is extremely thin, giving a short diffusion distance. In the blood, most carbon dioxide is transported not as dissolved gas but converted to bicarbonate ion (HCO₃⁻).

QUESTION:
Gas exchange between alveolar air and the blood occurs by:
items.explanation: Respiratory gases cross by passive diffusion, each following its own partial-pressure gradient—no energy expenditure is required.

items.question:
PASSAGE:

QUESTION:
Oxygen diffuses from the alveoli into the pulmonary capillary blood because:

choices.A: the blood has a higher PO₂ than the alveolar air.
choices.B: the alveolar air has a higher PO₂ than the arriving blood.
choices.C: oxygen is actively pumped against its gradient.

items.explanation: O₂ moves from the higher-PO₂ alveolar air to the lower-PO₂ blood, i.e., down its gradient.

QUESTION:
The very large total surface area of the alveoli primarily serves to:

choices.A: slow the rate of gas exchange.
choices.B: increase the rate of gas exchange.

items.explanation: A larger surface area increases the rate of diffusion (rate is proportional to area), maximizing gas exchange.

QUESTION:
In the blood, the majority of carbon dioxide is transported as:
items.explanation: Most CO₂ is carried as bicarbonate (formed via carbonic anhydrase in red blood cells), with smaller amounts dissolved or bound to hemoglobin.

The extremely thin alveolar–capillary membrane facilitates gas exchange by:

choices.B: decreasing the diffusion distance, allowing rapid diffusion.

items.explanation: A thin membrane shortens the diffusion path; diffusion rate is inversely related to distance, so exchange is faster.

items.question: During DNA replication, the enzyme responsible for unwinding and separating the two strands of the double helix is:
items.explanation: Helicase unwinds the helix at the replication fork; polymerase synthesizes new strands, and ligase joins fragments.

items.question: An animal cell is placed in a hypotonic solution (lower solute concentration than the cell's interior). By osmosis, the cell will:

choices.D: actively pump out water using ATP.

items.explanation: Water moves toward the higher solute concentration (into the cell), so the cell takes up water and swells (and may lyse without a wall).

items.question:
PASSAGE:
When light passes from one transparent medium into another, it changes speed and generally bends—a phenomenon called refraction. Each medium has an index of refraction n = c/v, where c is the speed of light in vacuum and v is its speed in the medium; a larger n means light travels more slowly in that medium. Snell's law, n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂, relates the angles to the indices. Light entering a denser (higher-n) medium bends toward the normal.
When light travels from a denser medium toward a less dense one, it bends away from the normal; beyond a particular "critical angle," it is reflected entirely back into the denser medium—total internal reflection. Medical instruments such as fiber-optic endoscopes exploit this effect to carry light along flexible fibers.

QUESTION:
As light passes from air into a denser medium such as glass (higher index of refraction), it bends:
items.explanation: Entering a higher-index medium slows the light and bends the ray toward the normal.

Snell's law (n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂) relates:

choices.A: the wavelength and frequency of light only.
choices.B: the angles of incidence and refraction to the indices of refraction.
choices.C: the intensity of light to its color.

items.explanation: Snell's law connects the incidence and refraction angles with the two media's refractive indices.

items.explanation: Since n = c/v, a higher n corresponds to a lower speed of light in the medium.

Total internal reflection can occur when light travels:

choices.A: from a less dense medium into a denser medium.
choices.B: from a denser medium toward a less dense medium at an angle beyond the critical angle.
choices.D: only when the two media have identical indices.

items.explanation: Total internal reflection requires light going from higher to lower index at an angle exceeding the critical angle, so it reflects entirely back.

Fiber-optic endoscopes transmit light along flexible fibers primarily by means of:
items.explanation: Optical fibers guide light by repeated total internal reflection along the fiber.

items.question: A magnetic field exerts a force on a charged particle only when the particle is:

choices.B: moving (with a velocity component perpendicular to the field).

items.explanation: The magnetic force is F = qv × B; it requires motion (a velocity component perpendicular to B). A stationary or neutral particle feels no magnetic force.

items.question: A reaction has a very large equilibrium constant (K ≫ 1). At equilibrium, this indicates that:

choices.D: the reaction is at exactly 50% completion.

items.explanation: K ≫ 1 means the equilibrium lies far toward products.

items.question:
PASSAGE:
Sociologists of health study how societies define and respond to illness. One key concept is medicalization: the process by which conditions or behaviors once regarded as ordinary, moral, or legal matters come to be defined and treated as medical problems requiring diagnosis and treatment. Critics note that medicalization can extend professional authority over more areas of life; supporters note it can reduce stigma and direct resources to those who suffer.
A related idea is the "sick role," described by Talcott Parsons: a person recognized as sick is temporarily exempted from normal social obligations but is, in return, expected to regard the condition as undesirable, to seek competent help, and to try to recover. More broadly, the social construction of illness holds that what counts as illness is shaped not only by biology but by social and cultural factors that vary across time and place.

QUESTION:
Medicalization is best defined as the process by which:

choices.A: medical conditions are reclassified as moral failings.
choices.B: nonmedical problems come to be defined and treated as medical conditions.
choices.C: all illnesses are eliminated through treatment.

items.explanation: Medicalization is the redefinition of nonmedical matters (behaviors, life stages, problems) as medical conditions to be diagnosed and treated.

QUESTION:
According to Parsons' concept of the "sick role," a recognized sick person is:

choices.A: permanently freed from all social obligations with no expectations.
choices.B: exempted from normal duties but expected to seek help and try to recover.
choices.D: required to continue all normal obligations.

items.explanation: Parsons' sick role grants temporary exemption from duties in exchange for viewing the illness as undesirable, seeking competent help, and trying to recover.

QUESTION:
Which of the following best illustrates medicalization?

choices.A: Treating a bacterial infection with antibiotics.
choices.B: Reclassifying ordinary shyness as a treatable medical disorder.
choices.C: Setting a broken bone in a cast.

items.explanation: Turning everyday shyness into a diagnosable disorder converts a nonmedical trait into a medical one—medicalization. The other options are straightforward medical treatments of clearly medical problems.

The "social construction of illness" perspective emphasizes that:

choices.A: illness is determined purely by biology.
choices.B: what counts as illness is influenced by social and cultural factors that vary over time.
choices.C: illness has no biological component whatsoever.

items.explanation: Social constructionism stresses that illness definitions are shaped by social and cultural context (varying over time/place), without denying biology.

QUESTION:
A behavior once treated as a moral failing in one era is later classified and treated as a medical condition. This shift is best described as an example of:
items.explanation: Reframing a former "moral failing" as a treatable medical condition is a textbook instance of medicalization.

items.question: The process by which a sensory receptor converts physical stimulus energy (such as light or sound) into a neural signal is called:
items.explanation: Transduction is the conversion of stimulus energy into neural (electrochemical) signals by receptors.

items.question: The "foot-in-the-door" technique increases compliance by:

choices.A: making a large request first, then a smaller one.
choices.B: obtaining agreement to a small request first, which raises the likelihood of agreeing to a larger one.

items.explanation: Foot-in-the-door secures a small initial agreement, which increases compliance with a subsequent larger request (contrast with the "door-in-the-face" technique).

items.question:
PASSAGE:
A map that left nothing out would be no map at all. To represent every stone, every blade of grass, every shifting shadow, it would have to be as large and as detailed as the territory itself, and then it would be useless—for the only thing harder to navigate than a landscape is a perfect copy of one. The map is helpful precisely because it discards almost everything, keeping only the few features that serve the traveler's purpose. Selection is not a defect of the map; it is the whole of its usefulness.
What is true of maps is true of every abstraction we think with. A statistic, a category, a scientific model, a rule of thumb—each functions by ignoring the overwhelming majority of what is true in order to render some narrow part of it graspable. The average household does not exist; the typical patient is a fiction; the frictionless plane is found nowhere in nature. Yet without such fictions we could not reason at all. To object that a model "leaves things out" is not to have caught it in an error. It is merely to have noticed that it is a model.
The danger, then, is not abstraction but the quiet forgetting of it: mistaking the map for the territory, the average for the actual person in front of you, the category for the particular case it was only ever meant to approximate. The competent user of any model lives with a doubleness that never fully resolves—holding, at once, that the model is indispensable and that it is, in some respect, always a falsehood. To lose either half of that thought is to be misled: by uselessness on the one side, by illusion on the other.

QUESTION:
The central claim of the passage is that:

choices.A: abstractions are flawed and should be avoided in favor of complete descriptions.
choices.B: abstractions are useful precisely because they omit, and the real danger is forgetting that they omit.
choices.C: a perfect, fully detailed map would be the most useful kind.
choices.D: statistics and models have no legitimate use.

items.explanation: The passage argues "Selection is... the whole of its usefulness" and that "the danger... is not abstraction but the quiet forgetting of it." (A), (C), and (D) are explicitly rejected.

items.question:
PASSAGE:
A map that left nothing out would be no map at all. To represent every stone, every blade of grass, every shifting shadow, it would have to be as large and as detailed as the territory itself, and then it would be useless—for the only thing harder to navigate than a landscape is a perfect copy of one. The map is helpful precisely because it discards almost everything, keeping only the few features that serve the traveler's purpose. Selection is not a defect of the map; it is the whole of its usefulness.
What is true of maps is true of every abstraction we think with. A statistic, a category, a scientific model, a rule of thumb—each functions by ignoring the overwhelming majority of what is true in order to render some narrow part of it graspable. The average household does not exist; the typical patient is a fiction; the frictionless plane is found nowhere in nature. Yet without such fictions we could not reason at all. To object that a model "leaves things out" is not to have caught it in an error. It is merely to have noticed that it is a model.
The danger, then, is not abstraction but the quiet forgetting of it: mistaking the map for the territory, the average for the actual person in front of you, the category for the particular case it was only ever meant to approximate. The competent user of any model lives with a doubleness that never fully resolves—holding, at once, that the model is indispensable and that it is, in some respect, always a falsehood. To lose either half of that thought is to be misled: by uselessness on the one side, by illusion on the other.

QUESTION:
The example of a map that "left nothing out" is used to make the point that:

choices.B: omission is what makes a map useful.
choices.C: maps should be as large as the territory.
choices.D: navigation is impossible with any map.

items.explanation: The oversized map shows that a representation is "helpful precisely because it discards almost everything"—omission is the source of usefulness.

QUESTION:
The statement "To object that a model 'leaves things out'... is merely to have noticed that it is a model" implies that:

choices.A: leaving things out is a correctable flaw in models.
choices.B: leaving things out is essential to what a model is, not a mistake.
choices.D: models and territories are the same thing.

items.explanation: The line means that omission defines a model; complaining about it isn't catching an error but "noticing that it is a model." (A) and (C) contradict this.

The "doubleness that never fully resolves" refers to the competent user's holding that a model is:

choices.B: both indispensable and, in some respect, always a falsehood.

items.question: A peptide bond forms between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the amino group of another. This condensation reaction releases:
items.explanation: Peptide bond formation is a dehydration (condensation) reaction that releases one water molecule.

items.question: Pepsin, a protein-digesting enzyme secreted in the stomach, functions optimally at a pH that is:
items.explanation: Pepsin works best in the strongly acidic stomach environment (around pH 2); neutral or basic pH inactivates it.

items.question: A frameshift mutation in a protein-coding gene most often results from:

choices.A: substitution of one nucleotide for another.
choices.B: insertion or deletion of a number of nucleotides not divisible by three.
choices.D: a change occurring only in non-coding DNA.

items.explanation: Inserting or deleting nucleotides in numbers not divisible by three shifts the reading frame, altering all downstream codons. A loss/gain of exactly three keeps the frame.

items.question: In eukaryotic cells, the citric acid (Krebs) cycle takes place in the:
items.explanation: The citric acid cycle occurs in the mitochondrial matrix (glycolysis is cytosolic; oxidative phosphorylation is at the inner membrane).

items.question: The covalent bond that links two monosaccharides together in a disaccharide is a:
items.explanation: Monosaccharides are joined by glycosidic bonds (formed by dehydration synthesis).

items.question: At standard temperature and pressure (STP), one mole of an ideal gas occupies a volume of approximately:
items.explanation: One mole of an ideal gas occupies about 22.4 L at STP (the molar volume).

items.question: A molecule with a central atom bonded to four other atoms and no lone pairs (such as CH₄) has a molecular geometry that is:
items.explanation: Four bonding pairs and no lone pairs give a tetrahedral geometry with ~109.5° bond angles.

items.question: A buffer solution, which resists changes in pH, is typically composed of:

choices.A: a strong acid and a strong base.
choices.B: a weak acid and its conjugate base (or a weak base and its conjugate acid).

items.explanation: A buffer is a conjugate acid–base pair (weak acid + its conjugate base, or weak base + its conjugate acid) that neutralizes added acid or base.

items.question: In a redox reaction, oxidation is defined as the:
items.explanation: Oxidation is loss of electrons (mnemonic: OIL RIG—Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain).

items.question: A reaction that is exothermic (ΔH < 0) and increases the system's entropy (ΔS > 0) will be spontaneous (ΔG < 0):
items.explanation: With ΔH < 0 and ΔS > 0, ΔG = ΔH − TΔS is negative at every temperature, so the reaction is always spontaneous.

items.question: The phenomenon in which exposure to misleading information after an event distorts a person's memory of that event is called the:
items.explanation: The misinformation effect occurs when post-event misleading information alters one's memory of the original event (studied extensively by Loftus).

items.question: In Kohlberg's theory of moral development, reasoning driven mainly by avoiding punishment and gaining rewards is characteristic of the:
items.explanation: Preconventional morality is based on consequences to the self—avoiding punishment and obtaining rewards.

items.question: Activation of the sympathetic nervous system most directly produces the:
items.explanation: The sympathetic branch mobilizes the body for "fight or flight" (raising heart rate, dilating pupils, etc.); the parasympathetic branch governs "rest and digest."

items.question: A group that an individual uses as a standard against which to evaluate themselves and their behavior is a:
items.explanation: A reference group provides the standard or benchmark people use to evaluate themselves and their circumstances.

items.question: A primary reinforcer differs from a secondary reinforcer in that a primary reinforcer:

choices.B: satisfies an innate biological need (e.g., food or water).
choices.C: has value only because it can be exchanged for other things.

items.explanation: A primary reinforcer is innately reinforcing because it meets a biological need; a secondary (conditioned) reinforcer—like money—gains its value through learned association.

items.question:
PASSAGE:
Nostalgia has a bad name. We treat it as a sentimental lie—a soft-focus distortion that paints the past in colors it never wore, editing out the boredom, the injustice, the ordinary unhappiness, and leaving only a golden haze. The charge is largely fair. Memory under the influence of longing is a notoriously unreliable witness; it remembers the summer and forgets the mosquitoes.
But the dismissal mistakes what nostalgia is. It is not, at bottom, a claim about the past—not a proposition asserting that things really were better then, which we could check against the record and refute. It is closer to a way of valuing: a signal, however garbled, of what we found worth keeping. When a person grows wistful over a cramped apartment they were glad to leave, they are not making a factual error about the rent; they are registering something—an intimacy, a hopefulness—that the later, larger life lost. The selections nostalgia makes, the very things it edits in and out, are not only its distortions. They are also its disclosures.
The harm begins when nostalgia is mistaken for history, or worse, conscripted into politics—when "make it as it was" treats a privately edited memory as a public program, and demands that the world be remade in the image of a past that, in the form longed for, never existed. Kept in its proper place, nostalgia is a private compass, pointing toward what one loves and has lost. Asked to do more—to legislate, to govern, to stand in for the record—it becomes a forgery passed off as a map.

QUESTION:
The central claim of the passage is that nostalgia:

choices.A: is simply a sentimental falsehood with no value.
choices.B: is not a factual claim about the past but a way of valuing, dangerous chiefly when mistaken for history or policy.
choices.C: is the most reliable form of memory.

items.explanation: The passage argues nostalgia "is not... a claim about the past" but "a way of valuing," and that "the harm begins when nostalgia is mistaken for history, or... politics." (A) and (D) are rejected; (C) is contradicted ("a notoriously unreliable witness").

The author's response to the common charge that nostalgia is a "sentimental lie" is to:

Based on the passage, which best describes the author's view of the proper role of nostalgia?

choices.A: A public program for remaking society.
choices.B: A private compass pointing toward what one loves and has lost.

items.question:
PASSAGE:
We picture creativity as an escape from constraint. The artist, on this romantic image, faces the blank page or the empty canvas in a condition of total freedom, and genius consists in what is conjured from that void. Form—the sonnet's fourteen lines, the strict answer a fugue must make to its own subject, the rules of a game—appears on this picture as a cage, a residue of less inventive ages from which the modern spirit has been liberated.
Yet this is rarely how makers describe the experience of making. For them the constraint is, more often, the very thing that makes invention possible. An unlimited field of possibility is not a help but a paralysis; the mind has nothing to push against, and so produces nothing. A limit changes that. By ruling out almost everything, it narrows the impossible vastness of the possible to a problem small enough to be worked, and the resistance of the rule—the search for a rhyme that must also make sense, the note the form forbids—is frequently what throws up the idea that mere freedom never would. The blank page is not liberty; it is a kind of dread. The imposed limit, far from a cage, is often a gift.
This is not a claim that any constraint will do, or that more is always better. A pointless rule, or a crushing one, deadens rather than quickens; not every limit is fertile. The useful constraint is the one that focuses without dictating—that fixes the terms of the problem while leaving the solution genuinely open, room enough for the unforeseen. Freedom in art, then, is poorly described as the absence of limits. It is better described as the right limits, well used.

QUESTION:
The central claim of the passage is that:

choices.B: constraints often enable creativity rather than merely limiting it, so freedom in art means the right limits well used.
choices.C: the more rules an artist follows, the better the art.
choices.D: form is an outdated cage that modern artists have rightly discarded.

items.explanation: The thesis is that constraint "is... the very thing that makes invention possible," concluding that freedom in art "is better described as the right limits, well used." (A) and (D) are the romantic view rejected; (C) is contradicted by the third paragraph.

The "romantic image" of creativity described in the first paragraph is presented as:

items.explanation: The romantic image (freedom from constraint, form as "a cage") is laid out so the author can answer, "Yet this is rarely how makers describe the experience."

The claim that "the blank page is not liberty; it is a kind of dread" is used to support the idea that:

choices.A: total freedom can paralyze rather than enable creation.

items.explanation: The line supports the claim that "an unlimited field of possibility is not a help but a paralysis"—total freedom can stall creation.

Which statement best expresses a qualification (concession) the author makes?

choices.A: Any constraint whatsoever improves creative work.

The closing phrase "the right limits, well used" conveys the author's view that artistic freedom consists in:

choices.A: removing every constraint from the creative process.
